events.text: The patient is a 58-year-old female named Margaret Choi. She has a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus diagnosed 4 years prior (on metformin), non-smoker, and works as a high school librarian. No family history of pancreatic cancer. BMI 22.4.

At age 58, the patient presented to her primary care physician with a 6-week history of new-onset, worsening mid-epigastric pain radiating to the back, unintentional weight loss of 12 lbs over 2 months, and progressive jaundice. She also noted a new onset of poorly controlled blood glucose despite unchanged metformin dosing. Physical exam revealed scleral icterus and mild epigastric tenderness without a palpable mass. She was referred urgently to gastroenterology.

At age 58, the patient underwent a multiphasic CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast (pancreatic protocol). Findings: A 3.8 cm hypoenhancing mass in the head/uncinate process of the pancreas. The tumor contacts the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) over approximately 180 degrees of circumference. The superior mesenteric vein (SMV) and portal vein confluence demonstrate focal abutment with loss of the fat plane over approximately 90 degrees without frank encasement. The common bile duct is dilated to 14 mm with associated intrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation. The main pancreatic duct is dilated to 6 mm. No discrete hepatic lesions. No peritoneal implants. No ascites. Peripancreatic lymph nodes measuring up to 1.1 cm in the peripancreatic region. Impression: 3.8 cm pancreatic head/uncinate mass with borderline resectable features based on SMA abutment >180 degrees and SMV abutment without occlusion. Findings are suspicious for pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. Biliary obstruction noted.

At age 58, the patient underwent endoscopic ultrasound (EUS)-guided fine needle biopsy of the pancreatic head mass. Pathology result: Core biopsy of pancreatic head mass. Histology: Moderately differentiated ductal adenocarcinoma of the pancreas. Tumor cells show desmoplastic stroma, infiltrating glands with irregular architecture, nuclear atypia, and prominent mucin production. Immunohistochemistry: CK7 positive, CK20 negative, MUC1 positive, MUC2 negative, CDX2 negative, Smad4 (DPC4) negative by IHC (loss of expression), consistent with pancreatic primary. Ki-67 approximately 35%. Diagnosis: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, moderately differentiated.

At age 58 years, the patient received a diagnosis of borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) of the pancreatic head/uncinate process. AJCC 8th edition clinical staging: T3 N1 M0, Stage III (borderline resectable by NCCN/AHPBA criteria due to SMA contact >180 degrees and SMV abutment). Site: Pancreatic head, ICD-O site code C25.0. Histology: Ductal adenocarcinoma, ICD-O histology code 8500/3. Summary stage: Regional. Relevant biomarkers: CA 19-9 = 1,248 U/mL (markedly elevated); CEA = 6.4 ng/mL (mildly elevated); SMAD4 protein loss by IHC. Tumor size: 3.8 cm. Confirmation: Positive histology via EUS-FNB. Biliary obstruction present requiring ERCP with biliary stenting.

At age 58, the patient was discussed at the multidisciplinary pancreatic tumor board. Consensus: borderline resectable PDAC. Recommendation: neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX chemotherapy with goal of tumor downsizing and establishment of resectability, followed by restaging and consideration of chemoradiation prior to attempted surgical resection. The patient underwent ERCP with placement of a covered self-expanding metal biliary stent to relieve obstructive jaundice. Total bilirubin prior to stenting was 7.2 mg/dL. Repeat total bilirubin 2 weeks post-stenting was 1.1 mg/dL. Patient was medically cleared for chemotherapy. Performance status: ECOG 1.

At age 58 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing (comprehensive tumor genomic profiling, Foundation Medicine FoundationOne CDx) performed on the EUS-FNB pancreatic adenocarcinoma specimen. Results: KRAS p.G12V mutation (allele frequency 42%), TP53 p.R248W mutation (allele frequency 38%), CDKN2A homozygous deletion (copy number loss, 0 copies), SMAD4 p.R361H mutation (allele frequency 35%), ARID1A p.Q1178* nonsense mutation (allele frequency 18%), STK11 p.L67fs*35 frameshift mutation (allele frequency 20%). Copy number alterations: MYC amplification (8 copies), GATA6 amplification (7 copies). No BRCA1 or BRCA2 pathogenic mutations detected. No NTRK1/2/3 fusions. No microsatellite instability (MSI-stable). Tumor mutational burden (TMB): 4 mutations/Mb (low). No homologous recombination deficiency signature. Mismatch repair: proficient (pMMR) by IHC (MLH1+, MSH2+, MSH6+, PMS2+). ERBB2: not amplified. No BRAF mutations. No ALK, ROS1, or RET rearrangements. Actionable findings: None with currently approved agents for pancreatic cancer; case referred for clinical trial matching. Germline reflexed testing recommended given CDKN2A deletion, patient declined germline testing at this time.

At age 58 years, the patient initiated neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX chemotherapy (modified mFOLFIRINOX) for borderline resectable pancreatic head adenocarcinoma. Regimen: Oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2 IV day 1, Leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV day 1, Irinotecan 150 mg/m2 IV day 1 (reduced dose given concern for toxicity), 5-Fluorouracil 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours, every 14 days. G-CSF (pegfilgrastim) prophylaxis administered with each cycle. Antiemetics: ondansetron, dexamethasone, aprepitant. Dose modifications anticipated as needed. Target: 8 cycles.

At age 58, following cycles 2-3 of FOLFIRINOX, the patient developed grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy in a stocking-glove distribution (paresthesias and tingling in bilateral hands and feet), grade 2 fatigue, and grade 2 nausea despite antiemetics. She also developed grade 1 alopecia. No febrile neutropenia. ANC nadir 1.2 x10^9/L (grade 2). Oxaliplatin dose was reduced by 20% beginning cycle 4 due to neuropathy progression. No dose reduction of 5-FU or irinotecan required. Cold-induced dysesthesia (grade 1) was present and managed with behavioral modifications.

At age 58, after 4 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX (interim restaging), the patient underwent repeat multiphasic CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol. Findings: The pancreatic head/uncinate mass now measures 3.1 cm (previously 3.8 cm), representing a 18% reduction in size. The degree of SMA abutment has decreased to approximately 120 degrees (previously 180 degrees). The SMV abutment is less apparent; the fat plane is partially restored. No new hepatic lesions. Peripancreatic lymph nodes are smaller, now measuring up to 0.8 cm. No new sites of disease. CA 19-9 at the time of this scan: 412 U/mL (down from 1,248 U/mL). Impression: Partial response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy. Interval decrease in pancreatic head mass with improved vascular relationship. Continue therapy as planned.

At age 58, during cycles 5-6 of mFOLFIRINOX, the patient developed grade 2 diarrhea (4-6 loose stools per day) managed with loperamide. She also developed grade 2 mucositis. Grade 2 peripheral neuropathy persisted. No dose reductions beyond the oxaliplatin reduction already in place. CA 19-9 measured after cycle 6: 187 U/mL, continuing downward trend. Tolerating therapy with moderate difficulty but patient motivated to complete planned 8 cycles.

At age 58, after completing 8 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX (restaging CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol). Findings: Pancreatic head/uncinate mass now measures 2.4 cm (previously 3.1 cm at midpoint; originally 3.8 cm). The tumor no longer abuts the SMA (fat plane restored circumferentially). The SMV-portal vein confluence demonstrates no evidence of direct tumor contact; the fat plane is fully restored. The common bile duct stent is in place with decompressed intrahepatic ducts. Peripancreatic lymph nodes are no longer enlarged, largest measuring 0.6 cm. No hepatic lesions. No peritoneal disease. CA 19-9 at time of scan: 85 U/mL. Impression: Continued partial response to neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX. Tumor no longer meets borderline resectable criteria. Patient now considered potentially resectable; recommend multidisciplinary reassessment and consideration of chemoradiation prior to surgery per protocol.

At age 58, patient was re-presented at the multidisciplinary pancreatic tumor board following completion of 8 cycles mFOLFIRINOX. Excellent response with CA 19-9 decline from 1,248 to 85 U/mL and partial response on imaging with resolution of vascular involvement. Patient is now potentially resectable. Per institutional protocol for borderline resectable cases with good response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy, recommendation is to proceed with consolidative chemoradiation (5-FU or capecitabine-based long-course radiation) prior to surgical resection, followed by restaging and surgical evaluation. Surgical oncology concurs with this approach. Patient agrees to proceed with chemoradiation. ECOG performance status: 1. Weight has stabilized after initial loss (net weight loss from diagnosis: 8 lbs).

At age 58 years, the patient received long-course concurrent chemoradiation for borderline resectable pancreatic head adenocarcinoma. Radiation: Stereotactic body radiation therapy (SBRT) was considered but given prior borderline resectable status and institutional preference, the patient received conventionally fractionated intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) to the pancreatic tumor bed and regional lymphatics. Total dose: 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions (1.8 Gy/fraction), 5 days/week for approximately 5.5 weeks. Concurrent chemotherapy: Capecitabine 825 mg/m2 orally twice daily on radiation days (radiosensitizer). Treatment completed without interruption. Acute radiation toxicities: Grade 2 radiation-induced nausea and vomiting (managed with ondansetron and metoclopramide), grade 2 fatigue, grade 1 radiation dermatitis over upper abdomen. No hospitalization required during radiation.

At age 58-59, restaging CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol performed approximately 6 weeks after completion of chemoradiation. Findings: Pancreatic head/uncinate mass now measures 2.2 cm (stable to minimally decreased from 2.4 cm post-chemotherapy). Marked desmoplastic reaction in the peripancreatic tissue is noted; this may overestimate or underestimate the true viable tumor extent. The SMA and SMV/portal vein confluence demonstrate maintained fat planes with no evidence of arterial or venous involvement. No hepatic lesions. No peritoneal implants. Peripancreatic lymph nodes remain non-enlarged. CA 19-9: 62 U/mL. Impression: Stable to minimal further response after chemoradiation consolidation. Maintained resectability. Recommend surgical evaluation for Whipple procedure. PET/CT obtained as additional staging: No FDG-avid hepatic or distant metastatic disease. Mild residual FDG uptake in pancreatic head (SUVmax 3.1), substantially reduced from baseline.

At age 59 years, the patient underwent a pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) performed by a high-volume pancreatic surgeon. Intraoperative findings: No peritoneal metastases, no hepatic metastases confirmed on direct inspection and intraoperative ultrasound. The SMV and portal vein were free of tumor involvement; a small segment of the SMV wall was resected en bloc with the specimen as a precaution (primary venous repair performed without graft). SMA dissection was completed with negative soft tissue margin. Procedure: En bloc resection of the pancreatic head, uncinate process, duodenum, gallbladder, distal common bile duct, and 15 cm of proximal jejunum. Pylorus-preserving technique. Regional lymphadenectomy performed. Reconstruction: Pancreaticojejunostomy (duct-to-mucosa), hepaticojejunostomy, duodenojejunostomy. A 5-French pancreatic duct stent was placed across the pancreaticojejunostomy. Two closed suction drains placed in the peripancreatic bed. Estimated blood loss: 600 mL. No intraoperative blood transfusion required. Operative time: 7 hours 20 minutes. Postoperative ICU admission for monitoring x 18 hours, then transferred to the hepatopancreatobiliary surgical floor.

At age 59, the patient had a surgical pathology result from the pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple) specimen. Gross: Pancreatic head/uncinate process with attached duodenum, distal common bile duct, and gallbladder. Cut surface reveals a 2.0 x 1.8 x 1.6 cm firm, white, stellate mass in the pancreatic head with extensive desmoplasia. Microscopic: Residual moderately differentiated ductal adenocarcinoma with extensive treatment effect (fibrosis, calcification, macrophage infiltration, acinar atrophy). Viable tumor present in approximately 30% of the lesion bed (College of American Pathologists tumor regression grade: Grade 2 - moderate response). Perineural invasion: Present. Lymphovascular invasion: Not identified. Margins: Pancreatic neck margin: Negative (0.4 cm clearance); Common bile duct margin: Negative; Posterior/retroperitoneal (uncinate/SMA) margin: Negative (0.2 cm clearance); Duodenal margin: Negative. Lymph nodes: 4 of 24 regional lymph nodes positive for metastatic adenocarcinoma (pN2). Pathologic stage (post-neoadjuvant, AJCC 8th ed): ypT2 ypN2 M0, Stage III. IHC on residual tumor: SMAD4 loss confirmed (DPC4 negative). R0 resection (all margins negative). Ki-67 on residual viable tumor: approximately 20%. Venous wall segment: Fibrous adventitial tissue, no tumor in vascular wall specimen.

At age 59, on postoperative day 5, drain amylase was markedly elevated at 8,400 U/L (>3x upper limit of normal on postoperative day 3: 2,900 U/L), consistent with a Grade B pancreatic fistula (postoperative pancreatic fistula, ISGPS 2016 criteria). The patient developed low-grade fever (38.2 degrees C), mild leukocytosis (WBC 13.2 x10^9/L), and increased serous-sanguineous drain output. CT abdomen showed a peripancreatic fluid collection at the hepaticojejunostomy anastomosis and around the pancreatic anastomosis without discrete abscess. Management: Drains were left in place and repositioned under CT guidance. Total parenteral nutrition (TPN) was initiated. Octreotide 150 mcg subcutaneous three times daily was started. The pancreatic fistula resolved slowly over 6 weeks with conservative management. Hospital stay was extended from planned 7 days to 18 days total. The patient was discharged home with an indwelling Jackson-Pratt drain and drain management instructions. No reoperation required. Delayed gastric emptying (DGE, Grade B) also occurred - patient required nasogastric tube for decompression for 8 days postoperatively; prokinetic agents (metoclopramide) used. Patient advanced to oral diet by postoperative day 14.

At age 59, at the 6-week post-Whipple follow-up visit, the JP drain was removed after drain amylase had normalized to 38 U/L over the preceding week. The patient had lost an additional 9 lbs since surgery (cumulative weight loss from diagnosis now approximately 20 lbs). She was started on pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT: Creon 72,000 lipase units with meals, 36,000 with snacks) for exocrine pancreatic insufficiency. She was also started on insulin (basal-bolus regimen) for new post-pancreatectomy diabetes mellitus. Performance status: ECOG 1-2. CA 19-9 at 6 weeks post-surgery: 28 U/mL (near normal). Recommendation: Proceed with adjuvant chemotherapy given pN2 disease, R0 resection, and SMAD4 loss (high-risk features). Plan for gemcitabine plus capecitabine (GemCap) for 6 months per ESPAC-4 trial data. Referral to dietitian and diabetes educator made.

At age 59 years, the patient initiated adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine (GemCap) chemotherapy for resected (ypT2 ypN2 M0) pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma following Whipple procedure. Regimen (per ESPAC-4): Gemcitabine 1000 mg/m2 IV days 1, 8, 15 of a 28-day cycle; Capecitabine 1660 mg/m2/day orally in two divided doses on days 1-21 of a 28-day cycle. Planned duration: 6 cycles (6 months). Antiemetic prophylaxis: ondansetron PRN. Dose reduced to 75% at initiation given borderline performance status, recent prolonged postoperative recovery, and significant weight loss. CA 19-9 at start of adjuvant therapy: 22 U/mL.

At age 59, during cycles 1-3 of adjuvant GemCap, the patient developed Grade 2 hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia) from capecitabine, with erythema, swelling, and tenderness of palms and soles, interfering with daily activities. Capecitabine was dose-reduced by 25% after cycle 2. She also developed Grade 2 cytopenias (ANC nadir 0.9 x10^9/L, platelet nadir 88 x10^9/L) requiring gemcitabine dose reduction to 75% on day 8 of cycles 2 and 3. Grade 1 fatigue was persistent. No febrile neutropenia. No grade 3 or 4 toxicities. Pancreatic enzyme replacement and insulin therapy continued. Nutritional status remained marginal; albumin 3.1 g/dL at cycle 3 assessment.

At age 59, after completion of cycle 3 of adjuvant GemCap (midpoint restaging), CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast was obtained. Findings: No evidence of local recurrence in the surgical bed. No suspicious hepatic lesions - two subcentimeter hepatic hypodensities (6 mm and 5 mm) are noted in segments VI and VII, too small to characterize definitively but not present on prior scans. Surgical clips are present in the expected operative bed. No peritoneal implants. No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. Lung: No pulmonary nodules or masses. CA 19-9 at time of scan: 41 U/mL (slight increase from 22 U/mL - borderline concerning). Impression: No definitive evidence of recurrent or metastatic disease. Two small indeterminate hepatic hypodensities, likely too small to characterize; follow-up with MRI liver recommended.

At age 59, MRI abdomen with hepatobiliary protocol (gadoxetate disodium/Eovist) was obtained for further characterization of the indeterminate hepatic lesions noted on CT. Findings: Two hepatic lesions identified in the right lobe - a 9 mm lesion in segment VI and a 7 mm lesion in segment VII. On hepatobiliary phase, both lesions demonstrate rim enhancement on arterial phase with washout appearance and hypointensity on hepatobiliary phase, morphology atypical for simple cysts or hemangiomas. These features are concerning for metastatic disease in the setting of known pancreatic adenocarcinoma history. No additional hepatic lesions. No evidence of surgical bed recurrence. CA 19-9 at time of MRI: 58 U/mL (rising trend). Impression: Two right hepatic lobe lesions with imaging characteristics highly suspicious for hepatic metastases from pancreatic adenocarcinoma. Recommend biopsy for confirmation prior to change in management.

At age 59, the patient underwent CT-guided core needle biopsy of the 9 mm segment VI hepatic lesion (after informed discussion of risks/benefits). Pathology: Core biopsy, liver segment VI. Histology: Metastatic adenocarcinoma, morphology and immunohistochemical profile consistent with pancreatic primary. Tumor cells arranged in irregular glands with desmoplastic stroma. IHC: CK7 positive, CK20 negative, CDX2 negative, CA19-9 positive, MUC1 positive, SMAD4 (DPC4) negative (loss of expression consistent with index pancreatic tumor). Diagnosis: Metastatic pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma to liver. This confirms metastatic disease (M1 liver) occurring during adjuvant chemotherapy.

At age 59, following biopsy confirmation of hepatic metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma, the patient was seen in oncology clinic for urgent management discussion. The case was reviewed at multidisciplinary tumor board. Impression: Stage IV (M1, liver metastases) pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, occurring during adjuvant GemCap chemotherapy, consistent with chemotherapy-refractory disease after prior FOLFIRINOX exposure and current GemCap. The patient has received both FOLFIRINOX and GemCap; options discussed include: (1) FOLFOX or oxaliplatin-based therapy, (2) clinical trial, (3) nanoliposomal irinotecan (nal-IRI) plus 5-FU/LV (NAPOLI-1 regimen), (4) best supportive care. Given prior oxaliplatin exposure and residual neuropathy, FOLFOX was deemed less favorable. Patient wishes to pursue active treatment. Plan: Transition to nal-IRI plus 5-FU/LV (Onivyde regimen), and concurrent enrollment in a clinical trial exploring second-line treatment in PDAC will be attempted. Palliative care consultation placed. Patient informed of disease status; she is accompanied by her husband and daughter. Goals of care discussion initiated.

At age 59 years, the patient initiated second-line palliative chemotherapy with nanoliposomal irinotecan (nal-IRI, Onivyde) 70 mg/m2 IV over 90 minutes day 1, plus leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV day 1, plus 5-fluorouracil 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours, every 14 days (NAPOLI-1 regimen). Prior GemCap was discontinued. Goals: Disease control, symptom management, and quality of life preservation. UGT1A1 genotyping performed prior to initiation: UGT1A1 *28/*28 (homozygous), representing increased risk for irinotecan-related toxicity (Grade 3-4 neutropenia and diarrhea). Dose reduced to 50 mg/m2 for nal-IRI per institutional protocol for UGT1A1 *28/*28 patients. CA 19-9 at start of second-line: 58 U/mL.

At age 59-60, during cycles 1-3 of nal-IRI/5-FU/LV, the patient experienced significant toxicity consistent with UGT1A1 *28/*28 genotype. Grade 3 diarrhea (>7 stools/day above baseline) occurred in cycle 2 requiring hospitalization for IV hydration and electrolyte repletion (2-day admission). Grade 3 neutropenia (ANC nadir 0.6 x10^9/L) occurred in cycle 2 without fever. No febrile neutropenia. Nal-IRI dose was further reduced to 35 mg/m2 and 5-FU dose reduced by 25% after cycle 2. Grade 2 fatigue remained persistent. Despite dose reductions, quality of life declined significantly (ECOG performance status worsened to 2-3 during cycle 3).

At age 59-60, restaging CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis after 4 cycles of nal-IRI/5-FU/LV. Findings: Hepatic metastases have increased in size and number. The segment VI lesion now measures 2.1 cm (previously 0.9 cm), the segment VII lesion measures 1.6 cm (previously 0.7 cm), and two new lesions are identified in segment IV (1.2 cm) and segment VIII (0.8 cm). No evidence of locoregional recurrence. No new peritoneal disease. No pulmonary lesions. CA 19-9 at time of scan: 312 U/mL (markedly elevated from 58 U/mL). Impression: Progressive disease on nal-IRI/5-FU/LV. Hepatic metastases have increased in size and number. Disease progression on second-line therapy confirmed.

At age 60, the patient was seen in oncology clinic after confirmed disease progression on second-line nal-IRI/5-FU/LV. ECOG performance status now 3 (capable of only limited self-care, confined to bed or chair >50% of waking hours). Significant ascites is now present (new). Serum albumin: 2.4 g/dL. Total bilirubin: 2.1 mg/dL. CA 19-9: 312 U/mL. She has received 3 lines of therapy (FOLFIRINOX, GemCap, nal-IRI/5-FU/LV). Genomic profile does not offer approved targeted options (KRAS G12V - not approved for KRAS inhibition in PDAC at time of treatment; pMMR/MSS; no BRCA1/2; no NTRK fusion; no TMB-high). Third-line chemotherapy options were discussed but given PS 3, worsening hepatic function, and patient/family preferences, the decision was made together to transition to hospice and best supportive care. The patient understands and accepts this recommendation. Inpatient palliative care and hospice evaluation requested. Opioid management optimized for pain control. Goals of care: comfort-focused.

At age 60, the patient was enrolled in home hospice approximately 18 months after initial diagnosis. She was managed with hydromorphone PCA for pain, haloperidol for nausea, and dexamethasone 2 mg daily for appetite stimulation and anti-emesis. She remained at home with her husband and daughter at bedside. The patient died peacefully at home approximately 6 weeks after transitioning to hospice, 19 months from her initial diagnosis of borderline resectable pancreatic adenocarcinoma. Cause of death: progressive metastatic pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma with hepatic failure and cachexia.

documents.text:
GASTROENTEROLOGY CONSULTATION NOTE

Patient: Margaret Choi
Age: 58 years old
Referring Physician: Primary Care (urgent referral)
Reason for Consultation: New-onset epigastric pain with radiation to the back, progressive jaundice, unintentional weight loss, and new poorly controlled hyperglycemia

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Choi is a 58-year-old woman referred urgently by her primary care physician for evaluation of a 6-week history of progressive mid-epigastric pain radiating to the back, new-onset jaundice, and a 12-pound unintentional weight loss over the past 2 months. She is accompanied today by her husband.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Choi is a previously healthy 58-year-old female librarian with a background of well-controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus who presents to gastroenterology for urgent evaluation of a constellation of symptoms highly concerning for a pancreatic process.

Approximately 6 weeks prior to this visit, she first noticed a dull, poorly localized discomfort in the mid-epigastric region. The pain has since become persistent and progressively more intense, now rating 5-6/10 at baseline, occasionally worsening to 8/10 with eating. A characteristic feature she endorses is radiation of the pain into her mid-back, which she describes as a boring, deep ache. The pain is not clearly colicky in nature, is not relieved by antacids, and does not improve with position changes, though she reports minimal relief when leaning forward. She denies hematemesis, melena, or frank hematochezia.

Over the same 6-week period, she has noticed progressive yellowing of the whites of her eyes, which was first noticed by her husband about 4 weeks ago. She reports that her urine has become distinctly dark (described as "tea-colored") and her stools have become pale and greasy over approximately the same timeframe. She denies prior episodes of jaundice. She has noted significant pruritus, predominantly on her extremities and trunk, over the past 2-3 weeks.

Most concerning to Ms. Choi is her weight loss. She reports an unintentional loss of approximately 12 pounds over the past 2 months without any changes in diet or activity level. She attributes some of this to reduced appetite, which she notes began around the same time as her pain. She does not recall any preceding viral illness, travel, sick contacts, or exposure to hepatotoxic agents or herbal supplements.

Of additional note, her primary care physician flagged a significant change in her glycemic control. Ms. Choi has been maintained on metformin for type 2 diabetes for the past 4 years, and her hemoglobin A1c had been stable in the 6.8-7.2% range. Over the past 2-3 months, however, her fasting glucose readings at home have been running consistently in the 200-280 mg/dL range despite no changes in her diet, activity level, or metformin dosing. She is not on insulin.

She denies prior episodes of pancreatitis, prior abdominal surgeries, history of gallstones, or prior liver disease. She denies any personal or family history of pancreatic cancer, hereditary pancreatitis, or hereditary cancer syndromes. She has no prior history of endoscopic evaluation. She has not had a recent colonoscopy within the recommended interval.

She has not taken any NSAIDs chronically. Alcohol use: None (lifelong non-drinker). Tobacco: Never smoker.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY

1. Type 2 diabetes mellitus - diagnosed approximately 4 years ago; previously well-controlled on metformin; A1c recently worsening as noted above
2. No prior history of pancreatitis
3. No prior history of cholecystitis or cholelithiasis
4. No prior malignancy
5. Hypertension - none
6. Thyroid disease - none
7. No history of inflammatory bowel disease

Past Surgical History:
- No prior abdominal or other major surgeries
- No prior endoscopic procedures (no prior EGD, colonoscopy, or ERCP)

---

FAMILY HISTORY

- Father: Coronary artery disease, died at age 74
- Mother: Type 2 diabetes mellitus, alive at age 83
- One sibling (brother): Healthy
- No family history of pancreatic cancer
- No family history of colon, ovarian, breast, or other gastrointestinal malignancies
- No known familial cancer syndromes (Lynch syndrome, BRCA-related, familial atypical mole-melanoma syndrome, Peutz-Jeghers, hereditary pancreatitis)

---

SOCIAL HISTORY

- Occupation: High school librarian; currently employed full-time
- Marital status: Married; supportive husband present at visit
- Living situation: Lives at home with husband
- Tobacco: Never
- Alcohol: None (lifelong abstainer)
- Illicit substances: None
- BMI: 22.4 kg/m2 (recent weight loss may have lowered this from prior baseline)
- Physical activity: Previously active (walks 30 minutes/day), now limited by fatigue and pain
- Functional status: Working full-time until approximately 3-4 weeks ago; currently taking leave of absence due to symptoms

---

ALLERGIES

- No known drug allergies
- No known food allergies

---

MEDICATIONS

1. Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily (type 2 DM; now subtherapeutic for glycemic control)
2. Multivitamin daily
3. No other prescription or over-the-counter medications
4. No herbal supplements or alternative remedies

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Positive:
- Mid-epigastric pain with back radiation (as above)
- Progressive scleral icterus and skin jaundice
- Dark urine and pale/acholic stools
- Significant pruritus
- Unintentional weight loss (12 lbs/2 months)
- Anorexia and early satiety
- Fatigue
- New-onset poorly controlled blood glucose

Negative:
- No fever or chills
- No hematemesis, melena, or hematochezia
- No nausea or vomiting (mild nausea occasionally with meals)
- No diarrhea or change in stool consistency (stools are pale/acholic but not liquid)
- No dysphagia or odynophagia
- No prior episodes of similar pain
- No palpitations or chest pain
- No shortness of breath
- No dysuria or urinary symptoms other than dark urine
- No skin rashes (aside from jaundice)
- No arthralgias
- No new lymphadenopathy noted by patient
- No night sweats

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs:
- Temperature: 37.1 degrees C
- Blood pressure: 124/78 mmHg
- Heart rate: 82 bpm, regular
- Respiratory rate: 14 breaths/min
- Oxygen saturation: 98% on room air
- Weight: 127 lbs (BMI approximately 22.4 kg/m2; patient reports pre-illness weight of approximately 139 lbs)

General: Well-appearing but visibly jaundiced female in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Appears slightly fatigued. Cooperative and engaged during history-taking.

HEENT: Scleral icterus is clearly present bilaterally. Conjunctivae non-injected. Oropharynx clear, no oral lesions. No cervical lymphadenopathy. No temporal wasting.

Neck: Supple. No palpable cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. No thyromegaly.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi. Good air movement.

Abdomen: Abdomen is soft. Mild to moderate epigastric tenderness to deep palpation, without guarding or rigidity. No rebound tenderness. Liver edge palpable approximately 2 cm below the right costal margin in the midclavicular line; liver is smooth and non-tender to palpation. No palpable gallbladder (Courvoisier's sign not clearly elicited but not definitively excluded on exam). Spleen not palpable. No palpable abdominal mass. No ascites on percussion (negative shifting dullness, no fluid wave). Bowel sounds present and normal.

Back: Mild tenderness to deep palpation over the mid-thoracic and upper lumbar paraspinal musculature bilaterally. No vertebral tenderness. No CVA tenderness.

Skin: Jaundiced. Mild excoriations on bilateral forearms and lower extremities consistent with pruritus. No spider angiomata. No palmar erythema. No caput medusae.

Extremities: No peripheral edema. No Virchow's node (left supraclavicular) or Sister Mary Joseph nodule identified. No clubbing or cyanosis.

Neurological: Alert and oriented. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No focal neurological deficits.

Lymph nodes: No palpable axillary, inguinal, or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (obtained by referring PCP, reviewed today)

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 8.4 x10^9/L (normal differential)
- Hemoglobin: 11.8 g/dL (mild normocytic anemia)
- Platelets: 324 x10^9/L

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Total bilirubin: 7.2 mg/dL (markedly elevated)

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:

Ms. Choi was initially diagnosed several months ago with borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) of the pancreatic head/uncinate process following evaluation for worsening mid-epigastric pain radiating to the back, unintentional weight loss of approximately 12 pounds, progressive jaundice, and new-onset poorly controlled blood glucose despite her baseline metformin therapy for type 2 diabetes mellitus. Pancreatic protocol CT at diagnosis demonstrated a 3.8 cm hypoenhancing mass in the pancreatic head/uncinate process with SMA contact over approximately 180 degrees and SMV abutment with loss of fat plane over roughly 90 degrees, meeting NCCN/AHPBA criteria for borderline resectability. EUS-guided fine needle biopsy confirmed moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. CA 19-9 at diagnosis was markedly elevated at 1,248 U/mL. CEA was mildly elevated at 6.4 ng/mL. Comprehensive genomic profiling (FoundationOne CDx) demonstrated KRAS G12V, TP53 R248W, CDKN2A homozygous deletion, SMAD4 R361H, ARID1A Q1178* nonsense, and STK11 frameshift mutations, with MYC and GATA6 amplifications. No BRCA1/2 mutations, no NTRK fusions, MSS/pMMR, and low TMB (4 mut/Mb). Given biliary obstruction at presentation (total bilirubin 7.2 mg/dL), ERCP with placement of a covered self-expanding metal biliary stent was performed prior to chemotherapy initiation with excellent decompression (bilirubin normalized to 1.1 mg/dL).

Following initial multidisciplinary tumor board discussion, she was initiated on modified FOLFIRINOX (mFOLFIRINOX) neoadjuvant chemotherapy: oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2 day 1, leucovorin 400 mg/m2 day 1, irinotecan 150 mg/m2 day 1 (reduced from 180 mg/m2 upfront for tolerability), and 5-FU 2400 mg/m2 over 46-hour continuous infusion, every 14 days, with pegfilgrastim support. Eight cycles were planned.

Her course was notable for several manageable toxicities. After cycles 2-3, she developed grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy in a stocking-glove distribution, grade 2 fatigue, grade 2 nausea, grade 1 alopecia, and cold-induced dysesthesias. Notably, no dose reductions were required for 5-FU or irinotecan beyond the initial irinotecan reduction. No febrile neutropenia occurred; ANC nadir was 1.2 x10^9/L (grade 2) managed with pegfilgrastim.

Interim restaging after 4 cycles showed meaningful response: the mass decreased from 3.8 cm to 3.1 cm (approximately 18% size reduction), SMA abutment decreased from approximately 180 degrees to 120 degrees, the SMV fat plane was partially restored, and peripancreatic nodes decreased to 0.8 cm. MULTIDISCIPLINARY TUMOR BOARD SUMMARY:

Ms. Choi's case was presented at the hepatopancreatobiliary multidisciplinary tumor board today, attended by pancreatic surgery, radiation oncology, gastrointestinal medical oncology, interventional radiology, pathology, and radiology. The consensus recommendations were as follows:

The patient has achieved an excellent response to 8 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX neoadjuvant chemotherapy. Radiographically, the tumor has decreased from 3.8 cm to 2.4 cm and, most significantly, vascular involvement has been eliminated — the SMA and SMV are now clear. The CA 19-9 has declined by more than 93%. The patient is now deemed potentially resectable. She was previously borderline resectable at diagnosis and thus the institutional protocol recommends proceeding with consolidative chemoradiation prior to surgical resection rather than proceeding directly to surgery. This approach is favored because: (1) consolidative chemoradiation offers additional local control and may further sterilize potential microscopic residual disease and regional lymphatics; (2) it provides additional time to observe systemic disease behavior and ensure no early distant metastases emerge; and (3) it is consistent with the standard institutional protocol for patients with borderline resectable disease who have responded to neoadjuvant chemotherapy.

1. Proceed with long-course concurrent chemoradiation to the primary tumor and regional lymphatics.
2. Radiation technique: intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT), approximately 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions (1.8 Gy per fraction), five days per week over approximately 5.5 weeks.
3. Concurrent radiosensitizing chemotherapy: capecitabine (Xeloda) 825 mg/m2 orally twice daily on radiation treatment days.
4. Following chemoradiation, allow approximately 6-8 weeks recovery, then perform restaging CT (and PET/CT) to confirm maintained resectability.
5. If restaging confirms maintained resectability without distant metastatic disease, proceed to surgical evaluation for pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure).
6. Surgical oncology is on board and will reassess the patient after chemoradiation and restaging.

RESTAGING CT ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH PANCREATIC PROTOCOL AND PET/CT

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Margaret Choi is a 58-year-old female with a history of borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the head/uncinate process (KRAS G12V, TP53, SMAD4 loss, CDKN2A deletion), initially staged cT3N1M0 (Stage III). She underwent neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX for 8 cycles with excellent serologic and radiographic response (CA 19-9 declined from 1,248 U/mL to 85 U/mL; primary tumor decreased from 3.8 cm to 2.4 cm with restoration of vascular fat planes). She subsequently completed consolidative long-course concurrent chemoradiation (IMRT 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions with concurrent capecitabine as radiosensitizer), completing treatment approximately 6 weeks prior to this examination. This study is obtained for restaging assessment prior to surgical evaluation. Prior biliary stent in place. CA 19-9 at time of current examination: 62 U/mL.

TECHNIQUE:
CT ABDOMEN/PELVIS: Multiphasic CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed using a dedicated pancreatic protocol. Imaging was acquired in the non-contrast, early arterial, late arterial (pancreatic parenchymal), and portal venous phases following intravenous administration of iohexol contrast (weight-based dosing). Multiplanar reformats (axial, coronal, and sagittal) were obtained and reviewed. Slice thickness: 2.5 mm. Comparison: CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol post-8 cycles mFOLFIRINOX.

18F-FDG PET/CT: Separately, the patient underwent whole-body 18F-FDG PET/CT for extended metabolic staging. Standard uptake values (SUV) were calculated. The patient fasted for a minimum of 6 hours prior to radiotracer injection. Blood glucose at time of injection: 118 mg/dL. Images acquired 60 minutes post-injection. Low-dose CT acquired for attenuation correction and anatomic localization.

FINDINGS - CT ABDOMEN/PELVIS:

Pancreas:
There is a heterogeneously hypovascular mass centered in the pancreatic head and uncinate process measuring approximately 2.2 cm in greatest transaxial dimension (previously 2.4 cm following completion of 8 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX; originally 3.8 cm at initial presentation). The interval change from 2.4 cm to 2.2 cm represents minimal further reduction and may reflect continued late treatment effect from chemoradiation or simply measurement variability. The overall morphology of the mass has remained largely stable compared with the post-FOLFIRINOX restaging examination. The lesion is poorly circumscribed with surrounding desmoplastic changes that produce a stellate, spiculated appearance of the peripancreatic fat, findings well-recognized after combined neoadjuvant chemotherapy and radiation. Notably, the conspicuity of the mass on the pancreatic parenchymal phase is somewhat diminished compared with the pre-treatment examination, which may in part reflect treatment-induced fibrosis reducing the tumor's hypovascularity relative to surrounding parenchyma.

Vascular Relationships:
The relationship between the tumor and the major peripancreatic vasculature has remained favorable relative to the post-chemotherapy evaluation and has not deteriorated following chemoradiation. The superior mesenteric artery (SMA) demonstrates a well-maintained fat plane circumferentially, with no evidence of tumor re-approximation, irregularity of the arterial wall, or soft tissue stranding along the SMA. No teardrop deformity of the SMA is observed. The superior mesenteric vein (SMV) and portal vein confluence demonstrate maintained luminal patency with no focal narrowing, contour deformity, or loss of the perivascular fat plane. There is no soft tissue cuffing around the portal vein. The celiac axis and its major branches (common hepatic artery, splenic artery) are patent and demonstrate no evidence of tumor involvement or encasement. In summary, the prior vascular encroachment that initially defined this lesion as borderline resectable has been durably addressed by neoadjuvant treatment and remains resolved.

Biliary System:
A covered self-expanding metal biliary stent is in situ within the common bile duct, unchanged in position. The intrahepatic bile ducts are decompressed and non-dilated. The main pancreatic duct in the body and tail appears mildly prominent but is unchanged from the post-FOLFIRINOX scan; there is no upstream ductal dilation attributable to worsening obstruction.

Liver:
The liver is normal in size and morphology. No focal hepatic lesions are identified. No hypervascular or hypovascular lesions are present on any phase of enhancement to suggest metastatic deposits. No biliary ductal dilatation in the hepatic parenchyma.

Spleen:
The spleen is normal in size (9.8 cm craniocaudal span) and homogeneous in attenuation. No splenic lesions.

Adrenal Glands:
Bilateral adrenal glands are unremarkable. No adrenal nodules or masses.

Kidneys:
The kidneys are symmetric in size with normal cortical thickness and enhancement. No hydronephrosis. No renal masses. Bilateral kidneys appear mildly heterogeneous on nephrographic phase, likely related to transient corticomedullary asymmetry; no discrete lesions.

Gastrointestinal Tract:
The stomach is unremarkable with no wall thickening. The duodenum is partially decompressed; no mucosal abnormality identified at the periampullary region on this examination. No small or large bowel obstruction. No bowel wall thickening to suggest radiation enteritis, though early submucosal edema in the peri-target small bowel loops immediately adjacent to the radiation field may be present and is a recognized acute sequela of radiation therapy to this region.

Peritoneum and Retroperitoneum:
No peritoneal nodularity, omental thickening, or soft tissue implants are identified. No ascites. No mesenteric or retroperitoneal soft tissue masses. The aorta and inferior vena cava are patent and normal in caliber.

Lymph Nodes:
Peripancreatic lymph nodes remain small and non-concerning in the current evaluation. The largest peripancreatic node measures approximately 0.6 cm in short axis, which is unchanged from the post-FOLFIRINOX restaging CT and substantially smaller than the 1.1 cm nodes observed at initial presentation. No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes are identified in the celiac, para-aortic, portocaval, or mesenteric nodal stations.

Bones (included in field):
Visualized bony structures, including the lower thoracic and lumbar vertebrae, pelvis, and proximal femoral heads, demonstrate no lytic or sclerotic lesions suspicious for osseous metastatic involvement. Degenerative changes in the lumbar spine are present and age-appropriate.

Lung Bases (included in field):
Small bilateral dependent atelectasis is noted at the posterior lung bases. No pleural effusions. No discrete pulmonary nodules identified at the lung bases within the imaged field.

FINDINGS - 18F-FDG PET/CT:

Pancreas:
There is mildly elevated FDG uptake within the pancreatic head and uncinate process corresponding to the known primary tumor site. The FDG avidity is notably decreased compared with what would be anticipated at initial staging, consistent with substantial metabolic treatment response following neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX and consolidative chemoradiation. The residual uptake demonstrates a maximum standardized uptake value (SUVmax) of 3.1. This level of uptake is only modestly above background hepatic activity (SUVmean liver approximately 2.3) and is substantially lower than would be expected for an untreated pancreatic head adenocarcinoma of this size. It is difficult to distinguish with certainty whether this mild residual uptake reflects low-level viable residual tumor, post-treatment inflammatory and fibrotic tissue, or reactive changes in the context of recent chemoradiation. The pattern is nonetheless markedly improved.

Liver:
No FDG-avid hepatic lesions are identified. No focal areas of increased uptake to suggest hepatic metastatic deposits. Hepatic background activity is homogeneous.

Peritoneum:
No FDG-avid peritoneal nodularity or omental deposits are identified. No evidence of peritoneal carcinomatosis on metabolic imaging.

Lymph Nodes:
No FDG-avid regional or distant lymph node metastases identified. No pathologic uptake in celiac, para-aortic, portocaval, mediastinal, or supraclavicular nodal stations.

Bones:
No FDG-avid osseous metastatic lesions. Physiologic uptake in red marrow distribution.

Lungs and Mediastinum:
No FDG-avid pulmonary nodules or masses. No mediastinal or hilar lymph node FDG uptake above background. No pleural effusions with metabolic activity.

Other:
No FDG-avid lesions identified elsewhere in the surveyed body to suggest occult distant metastatic disease. Physiologic FDG distribution in bowel, bladder, and kidneys.

IMPRESSION:

1. Pancreatic head/uncinate mass measures approximately 2.2 cm, minimally decreased to stable compared with the 2.4 cm measurement following 8 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX. The overall trajectory from initial presentation (3.8 cm) to post-chemotherapy (2.4 cm) to current (2.2 cm) reflects a meaningful and sustained treatment response. The minimal interval change after chemoradiation is typical in this setting, where fibrosis and desmoplastic reaction often dominate the residual imaging appearance. The treatment effect and desmoplastic stroma present some inherent limitation in accurately gauging residual tumor burden.

2. Favorable vascular anatomy is maintained. The SMA fat plane is preserved circumferentially, and the SMV/portal vein confluence demonstrates no contour deformity or tumor encroachment. By cross-sectional imaging criteria, there is no evidence of arterial or venous involvement that would preclude surgical exploration.

3. No hepatic metastases or peritoneal disease identified by either CT or PET/CT.

4. No FDG-avid distant metastatic disease on whole-body PET/CT. Mild residual FDG uptake in the pancreatic head (SUVmax 3.1), substantially reduced from expected pre-treatment levels, consistent with significant metabolic treatment response.

5. Biliary stent in place with decompressed biliary system.

6. No pathologically enlarged regional or distant lymph nodes.

Overall, the findings are consistent with maintained partial response and sustained resectability following completion of neoadjuvant chemotherapy and consolidative chemoradiation. The absence of distant metastatic disease on metabolic imaging supports proceeding with surgical evaluation.

**CHIEF COMPLAINT**
Margaret Choi is a 59-year-old female presenting for her 6-week post-operative follow-up and adjuvant chemotherapy planning visit following pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) for pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. She is accompanied today by her husband.

---

**HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS**

Ms. Choi is a 59-year-old female with a history of borderline resectable pancreatic head ductal adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12V, TP53 R248W, CDKN2A deletion, SMAD4 loss) who presented originally with obstructive jaundice, epigastric pain, and unintentional weight loss approximately 8 months ago. Her oncologic course to date is summarized below.

At presentation, she had a 3.8 cm pancreatic head/uncinate mass with SMA abutment of approximately 180 degrees and SMV abutment without frank occlusion, staged as cT3N1M0 (borderline resectable by NCCN/AHPBA criteria). CA 19-9 was 1,248 U/mL and CEA was mildly elevated. She was diagnosed via EUS-guided fine needle biopsy confirming moderately differentiated PDAC with SMAD4 loss by IHC. Biliary obstruction was treated with ERCP and covered metal biliary stent placement, with normalization of bilirubin.

After multidisciplinary tumor board review, she received neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX (8 cycles) with a 20% oxaliplatin dose reduction beginning cycle 4 for grade 2 peripheral neuropathy. She achieved a partial response with the tumor shrinking from 3.8 cm to 2.4 cm, CA 19-9 declining to 85 U/mL, and resolution of vascular involvement (fat planes restored around the SMA and SMV). She subsequently received consolidative chemoradiation with IMRT (50.4 Gy in 28 fractions) with concurrent capecitabine 825 mg/m2 twice daily on radiation days. Post-chemoradiation restaging showed the mass stable at 2.2 cm with maintained resectability and a CA 19-9 of 62 U/mL. PET/CT showed no FDG-avid distant metastatic disease.

She then underwent a pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy performed by our HPB surgical colleague. Intraoperatively, the SMV and portal vein were free of tumor; a small segment of the SMV wall was resected en bloc with primary venous repair. No peritoneal or hepatic metastases were found on direct inspection or intraoperative ultrasound. Final surgical pathology demonstrated a 2.0 cm residual tumor (ypT2 ypN2 M0, Stage III by AJCC 8th edition post-neoadjuvant staging), with approximately 30% viable tumor (CAP regression grade 2 -- moderate response), perineural invasion present, lymphovascular invasion absent, and all surgical margins negative (R0; pancreatic neck margin 0.4 cm, SMA/retroperitoneal margin 0.2 cm). Four of 24 regional lymph nodes were positive for metastatic adenocarcinoma (pN2). SMAD4 loss was confirmed on the residual viable tumor.

Her postoperative course was complicated by a Grade B postoperative pancreatic fistula (ISGPS 2016 criteria), with drain amylase peaking at 8,400 U/L on postoperative day 5. She also developed Grade B delayed gastric emptying requiring nasogastric tube decompression for 8 days. She was managed conservatively with prolonged drain maintenance (with repositioning under CT guidance), total parenteral nutrition, and octreotide. She was discharged on postoperative day 18 with an indwelling Jackson-Pratt drain in place. She did not require reoperation.

Today is her 6-week post-discharge follow-up. She reports that the JP drain output has been minimal and serous in character over the past 1-2 weeks, with drain amylase most recently checked and found to be 38 U/L (normalized). She reports progressive improvement in energy and oral intake since discharge, though she continues to struggle significantly with appetite and has lost an additional 9 lbs since discharge. She describes intermittent loose, fatty-appearing stools (2-4 per day) that she has noticed since advancing to full oral diet, consistent with steatorrhea. She reports blood glucose readings ranging widely from 85 to 280 mg/dL at home on her current regimen, with difficulty managing hypoglycemic episodes. She denies fever, chills, significant abdominal pain, jaundice, or wound issues. She reports persistent mild bilateral hand and foot paresthesias, which she attributes to residual neuropathy from oxaliplatin -- somewhat improved compared to her worst point during chemotherapy. She denies new nausea or vomiting. She is eager to understand the next steps in her care and is motivated to discuss adjuvant therapy.

**ASSESSMENT AND PLAN**

Ms. Margaret Choi is a 59-year-old woman with resected pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (ypT2 ypN2 M0, Stage III, post-neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX and chemoradiation followed by R0 Whipple), now 6 weeks post-discharge from a complicated hospitalization. Her surgical recovery is progressing, with resolution of her pancreatic fistula (drain removed today) and improving functional status. She has several important management issues to address at this visit:

**1. Adjuvant Chemotherapy Planning**
Ms. Choi has significant high-risk features that strongly favor adjuvant chemotherapy:
- pN2 disease (4/24 positive nodes)
- R0 but narrow retroperitoneal/SMA margin (0.2 cm)
- SMAD4 loss (associated with a higher propensity for distant metastatic spread and poor prognosis in PDAC)
- Residual viable tumor at 30% (CAP grade 2 -- moderate but not complete or near-complete response)

Given her prior treatment with FOLFIRINOX and concurrent capecitabine with radiation, the most appropriate adjuvant regimen is gemcitabine plus capecitabine (GemCap), as validated in the ESPAC-4 trial. We discussed this at length with the patient and her husband. She understands that adjuvant GemCap in this setting represents the standard of care for improving disease-free and overall survival following resected pancreatic cancer, and that her substantial residual node-positive disease underscores the importance of completing therapy. I reviewed that GemCap would consist of gemcitabine 1000 mg/m2 IV on days 1, 8, and 15 of a 28-day cycle, combined with oral capecitabine daily on days 1-21. We plan for 6 cycles (approximately 6 months).

Given her current ECOG PS of 1-2, ongoing nutritional challenges (albumin 3.0 g/dL), and the prolonged and complicated postoperative course, I plan to initiate adjuvant GemCap at a dose-reduced starting level (75% of full dose) with close monitoring and re-escalation as tolerated. I want to allow a minimum of 8-10 weeks from time of surgery before initiating, to optimize her nutritional and functional status. The plan is to recheck her weight, albumin, and performance status at a dedicated pre-treatment assessment visit in approximately 2-3 weeks, and if she is meeting nutritional and recovery benchmarks, we will initiate therapy at that time.

I discussed common side effects of GemCap, including fatigue, myelosuppression (low blood counts), nausea, and specifically the risk of hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia) from capecitabine. I advised her to use urea-based emollient creams on her hands and feet beginning at the start of treatment as prophylaxis for hand-foot syndrome. I counseled her to avoid tight footwear and prolonged pressure on palms and soles.

**2. CA 19-9 Trend**
CA 19-9 is now 28 U/mL, which is near-normal (normal <37 U/mL) and represents a dramatic decline from 1,248 U/mL at diagnosis. This is an encouraging finding consistent with R0 resection without evidence of gross residual disease. I cautioned the patient that CA 19-9 has limitations as a biomarker and that surveillance imaging will be the primary modality for monitoring. We will track CA 19-9 at each treatment visit.

**3. Exocrine Pancreatic Insufficiency / PERT Optimization**
She is experiencing steatorrhea (fatty, loose stools 2-4 per day) and has lost a further 9 lbs since discharge, indicating inadequate enzyme replacement and/or ongoing pancreatic insufficiency. I am increasing her Creon dose to 72,000 lipase units with each main meal and 36,000 units with snacks or smaller meals. I counseled her to take Creon at the start of and during meals (not after) and that fat restriction is not recommended as it further impairs caloric intake. A dietary referral has been placed with our oncology dietitian who specializes in patients with pancreatic resections.

I have also requested a 72-hour fecal fat collection to document severity of malabsorption and to have a baseline prior to adjuvant therapy. Fecal elastase-1 level will also be checked.

**4. Post-Pancreatectomy Diabetes Mellitus**
She has new insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus following near-total loss of pancreatic endocrine reserve after Whipple procedure (combined with her pre-existing type 2 diabetes). HbA1c 8.1% at this visit. Her blood glucose readings at home range widely (85-280 mg/dL) with some hypoglycemic episodes, which is characteristic of post-pancreatectomy "brittle" diabetes given loss of glucagon secretion as well. Metformin has been discontinued. She is currently on insulin glargine 18 units nightly and Humalog sliding scale.

I have placed a referral to endocrinology and a certified diabetes educator (CDCES) for intensive management given the complexity of this diabetes type. I counseled her regarding hypoglycemia recognition and management (including glucagon kit prescription given loss of counter-regulatory response). During GemCap, steroid premedication is not part of the standard regimen, which will simplify glucose management.

**5. Nutritional Support**
Albumin 3.0 g/dL with continued weight loss (cumulative ~20 lbs from diagnosis) is concerning. I stressed the importance of high-calorie, high-protein oral intake with frequent small meals. Nutritional supplement drinks (e.g., Ensure Plus or Boost HP) were recommended between meals. I will reassess whether enteral nutritional support (nasojejunal feeding) might be necessary if she continues to lose weight prior to adjuvant therapy initiation. Adequate nutritional status is essential for tolerating chemotherapy, and I will not initiate GemCap until we have documented weight stability or improvement and albumin is trending toward 3.2-3.5 g/dL. Vitamin D supplementation increased to 4000 IU daily given level of 22 ng/mL; fat-soluble vitamin panel pending.

**6. Peripheral Neuropathy**
Residual grade 1-2 bilateral peripheral sensory neuropathy in a length-dependent distribution, attributable to prior oxaliplatin exposure. Symptoms are improving. GemCap does not carry significant additional neuropathy risk. We discussed that neuropathy may continue to gradually improve over the coming months, though may not fully resolve. She is managing functionally at present. Recommended physical therapy referral if neuropathy interferes with balance or falls.

**7. JP Drain Removal**
The drain amylase has been stable at 38 U/L (below 3x upper limit of normal) on serial checks over the past week, confirming resolution of the pancreatic fistula. The Jackson-Pratt drain was removed in clinic today without complication. She tolerated removal well. Drain site care instructions provided; she was instructed to contact our office if she develops fever, site erythema, increased drainage, or pain.

**8. Anemia**
Hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL, likely multifactorial (postoperative dilutional, nutritional, possibly gastrointestinal). Iron studies and reticulocyte count ordered. Will reassess prior to initiating GemCap and correct any deficiencies.

**Return Visit / Follow-up Plan:**
- Pre-adjuvant therapy assessment visit in approximately 2-3 weeks to assess nutritional status, weight, albumin, CBC, and performance status; if meeting parameters, initiate GemCap cycle 1 at that time
- Lab work at that visit: CBC with differential, CMP, LFTs, CA 19-9, CEA, prealbumin, albumin, iron studies, fecal elastase-1
- Endocrinology referral placed (urgent)
- Oncology dietitian referral placed
- Diabetes educator referral placed
- Baseline CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast to be obtained at approximately 3 months post-surgery as surveillance and adjuvant treatment monitoring baseline
- Patient instructed to call with any fever >38 degrees C, severe diarrhea, uncontrolled nausea/vomiting, worsening abdominal pain, or hypoglycemic episode requiring glucagon

Time spent: 60 minutes (including history, exam, counseling, and coordination of care).

MULTIPHASIC CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
PANCREATIC PROTOCOL

CLINICAL INDICATION:
58-year-old female with a 6-week history of progressive mid-epigastric pain radiating to the back, unintentional weight loss of approximately 12 lbs over 2 months, new-onset jaundice with scleral icterus on examination, and new poorly controlled blood glucose despite stable metformin therapy. Referred by gastroenterology for evaluation of suspected pancreatic pathology. CA 19-9 markedly elevated at 1,248 U/mL (obtained prior to scan). No prior abdominal imaging available for comparison.

TECHNIQUE:
Multiphasic CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed on a 128-slice multidetector CT scanner following the administration of 125 mL of intravenous iohexol contrast at a rate of 4 mL/sec. Imaging was acquired in the late arterial (pancreatic parenchymal) phase at approximately 45 seconds, portal venous phase at approximately 70 seconds, and delayed phase at 180 seconds following contrast injection. Axial sections were acquired at 0.625 mm collimation with multiplanar reconstructions in coronal and sagittal planes. Thin-section curved planar reformats through the pancreatic duct and along the superior mesenteric artery and vein were generated for vascular assessment. Oral contrast was not administered.

FINDINGS:

Pancreas:
There is a 3.8 cm ill-defined, hypoenhancing mass centered in the head and uncinate process of the pancreas. On the pancreatic parenchymal phase, the lesion is markedly hypovascular relative to the surrounding normal pancreatic parenchyma, with absent internal arterial enhancement — a pattern characteristic of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. The mass demonstrates subtle heterogeneous low attenuation on all phases without discrete necrosis or internal calcification. The lesion has poorly defined margins and an infiltrative border with surrounding peripancreatic fat stranding, consistent with extrapancreatic extension. The body and tail of the pancreas are atrophic proximal to the mass, consistent with upstream parenchymal atrophy from ductal obstruction. The main pancreatic duct in the body and tail is dilated, measuring up to 6 mm in diameter, with abrupt caliber change at the level of the mass (double-duct sign).

Biliary System:
The common bile duct is markedly dilated, measuring up to 14 mm in the distal common bile duct just proximal to the level of the mass, with obstruction at the intrapancreatic segment. The intrahepatic bile ducts are diffusely dilated throughout both hepatic lobes. The gallbladder is distended and fluid-filled without wall thickening or pericholecystic fluid. No choledocholithiasis is identified. The findings are consistent with obstructive jaundice secondary to the pancreatic head mass.

Vascular Relationships:
Superior mesenteric artery (SMA): The pancreatic mass contacts the right lateral and posterior wall of the SMA over an arc estimated at approximately 180 degrees of its circumference. There is loss of the normal fat plane between the tumor and the vessel wall along this arc. No frank encasement is identified and luminal narrowing or deformity of the SMA is not appreciated. The celiac axis and its major branches (splenic artery, common hepatic artery, left gastric artery) are patent without involvement. The proper hepatic artery and gastroduodenal artery are preserved.

Superior mesenteric vein (SMV) / Portal vein confluence: The SMV demonstrates focal abutment with the right anterolateral margin of the mass over an arc of approximately 90 degrees. The fat plane along this short segment is effaced; however, the venous lumen is preserved without contour deformity, narrowing, or thrombosis. The portal vein confluence is similarly abutted, without frank encasement or occlusion. No SMV or portal vein thrombosis is identified. The splenic vein is patent.

Liver:
The liver is normal in size, morphology, and attenuation. No discrete hepatic lesions are identified. No focal hypodensities on portal venous or delayed phase imaging to suggest hepatic metastases. No hepatic arterial phase hypervascular lesions.

Spleen:
Normal size and homogeneous parenchymal enhancement. No splenic lesions.

Adrenal Glands:
Bilateral adrenal glands are unremarkable without nodular thickening or mass lesions.

Kidneys:
Both kidneys are normal in size and enhancement. No hydronephrosis, calculi, or renal masses. No perinephric abnormality.

Gastrointestinal Tract:
The stomach, visualized portions of the small bowel, and colon appear normal without evidence of obstruction, wall thickening, or mucosal abnormality. No bowel dilation.

Lymph Nodes:
Several mildly prominent peripancreatic lymph nodes are present in the peripancreatic and periportal soft tissue, the largest measuring 1.1 cm in short-axis dimension in the peripancreatic region. Additional subcentimeter nodes are present along the hepatoduodenal ligament and in the aortocaval region, which are below the threshold for definitive pathologic enlargement by size criteria alone. No bulky retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. No pelvic lymphadenopathy.

Peritoneum and Mesentery:
No peritoneal implants, omental nodularity, or mesenteric masses. No ascites. The peritoneal surfaces appear smooth and without discrete nodularity.

Pelvis:
No pelvic mass, free fluid, or pelvic lymphadenopathy. Uterus and ovaries are within normal limits for age.

Osseous Structures:
No lytic or sclerotic lesions suspicious for osseous metastatic disease. No compression fractures. Mild degenerative changes of the lumbar spine noted.

IMPRESSION:

1. 3.8 cm hypoenhancing mass in the head and uncinate process of the pancreas, with imaging characteristics strongly suspicious for pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. Double-duct sign (dilated common bile duct to 14 mm with dilated main pancreatic duct to 6 mm) and associated pancreatic parenchymal atrophy upstream are present, consistent with ductal obstruction by the mass.

2. With respect to resectability, the tumor contacts the SMA over approximately 180 degrees of its circumference without frank encasement or arterial deformity, representing a critical vascular relationship. The SMV and portal vein confluence demonstrate focal abutment with effacement of the fat plane over a short arc of approximately 90 degrees, without venous occlusion or thrombosis. These findings are consistent with borderline resectability by current surgical criteria.

3. Obstructive biliary dilatation is present, with the common bile duct dilated to 14 mm and associated intrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation, attributable to direct compression by the pancreatic head mass.

4. Mildly prominent peripancreatic lymph nodes measuring up to 1.1 cm; these may reflect reactive changes or early nodal involvement and cannot be reliably characterized on CT alone.

5. No discrete hepatic metastases identified. No peritoneal implants. No ascites. No distant metastatic disease evident on this study.

Correlation with serum tumor markers, clinical examination, and tissue sampling is strongly recommended. Multidisciplinary surgical and oncologic evaluation is advised to determine candidacy for surgical resection versus neoadjuvant therapy.

COMMENT:
The histomorphologic and immunohistochemical findings in this core needle biopsy of a right hepatic lobe mass are diagnostic of metastatic adenocarcinoma. The immunophenotype is characteristic of a pancreatobiliary primary: CK7 positive, CK20 negative, CDX2 negative, with MUC1 and CA19-9 positivity. Most notably, complete loss of SMAD4 (DPC4) nuclear expression is identified in the neoplastic cells, with preserved staining in surrounding stromal and hepatic cells, confirming this as a true loss rather than a technical artifact. This pattern of SMAD4 loss is highly characteristic of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma and is concordant with the SMAD4 p.R361H somatic mutation detected by comprehensive NGS (FoundationOne CDx) in the primary tumor at initial diagnosis, as well as the DPC4 loss confirmed by IHC in the index EUS-FNB specimen and subsequent Whipple resection specimen. A colorectal primary is effectively excluded by the absence of CK20, CDX2, and SATB2 expression. A cholangiocarcinoma primary is possible on IHC grounds alone, but is considered unlikely given the known pancreatic primary, the absence of biliary lesions on serial imaging, and the concordant SMAD4 loss. Hepatocellular carcinoma is excluded by negative HepPar-1, Arginase-1, and GPC3 with intact non-neoplastic hepatocyte staining. A neuroendocrine neoplasm is excluded by negative synaptophysin and chromogranin. The high Ki-67 labeling index (approximately 40%) in the metastatic deposit is somewhat elevated compared to the Ki-67 of approximately 20% noted in residual viable tumor at the time of Whipple resection, suggesting possible clonal selection of more proliferative disease in the metastatic setting, though Ki-67 assessment on small biopsy specimens has inherent sampling variability.

In the appropriate clinical context of a patient with known SMAD4-deficient pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma who developed rising CA 19-9 and new hepatic lesions during adjuvant chemotherapy, the findings confirm hepatic metastasis from pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (M1 disease). This represents progression to stage IV disease occurring during adjuvant GemCap therapy.

Correlation with clinical and radiologic findings is confirmed.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Choi is a 59-year-old female with a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus and a known diagnosis of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) of the pancreatic head/uncinate process, originally staged as borderline resectable (cT3 N1 M0, Stage III) at initial presentation approximately 13 months ago. She presented today with her husband and daughter for an urgent management discussion following the recent biopsy confirmation of hepatic metastatic disease.

To briefly summarize her prior oncologic course: she was initially found to have a 3.8 cm hypoenhancing pancreatic head/uncinate mass with SMA abutment greater than 180 degrees and SMV contact, prompting classification as borderline resectable at her initial multidisciplinary tumor board presentation. She underwent biliary stenting via ERCP for obstructive jaundice (pre-stent total bilirubin 7.2 mg/dL, normalized post-stent). Her tissue diagnosis confirmed moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma; molecular profiling (FoundationOne CDx) revealed KRAS p.G12V, TP53 p.R248W, CDKN2A homozygous deletion, SMAD4 p.R361H mutation, ARID1A p.Q1178*, and STK11 p.L67fs frameshift. MSS/pMMR. TMB 4 mut/Mb. No BRCA1/2 pathogenic mutations. No actionable targets by standard-of-care criteria.

She received 8 cycles of modified FOLFIRINOX (mFOLFIRINOX) as neoadjuvant chemotherapy with excellent biochemical and radiologic response: CA 19-9 dropped from 1,248 U/mL at diagnosis to 85 U/mL after 8 cycles, and the pancreatic head mass decreased from 3.8 cm to 2.4 cm with restoration of the SMA and SMV fat planes. She then received consolidative chemoradiation (IMRT 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions with concurrent capecitabine as radiosensitizer), completing this without interruption. Post-chemoradiation restaging imaging confirmed maintained resectability with CA 19-9 of 62 U/mL and no distant disease on PET/CT (mild residual FDG uptake in the pancreatic head, SUVmax 3.1).

She underwent a pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) approximately 7 months ago. The procedure was technically successful with R0 resection (all margins negative, closest margin 0.2 cm at the uncinate/SMA margin). Final surgical pathology revealed ypT2 ypN2 M0, Stage III disease with 4/24 lymph nodes positive for metastatic adenocarcinoma and moderate treatment response (College of American Pathologists Grade 2, approximately 30% viable residual tumor). Her postoperative course was complicated by a Grade B pancreatic fistula (drain amylase peaking at 8,400 U/L) and Grade B delayed gastric emptying, managed conservatively with prolonged drain placement, TPN, octreotide, and prokinetics. She was ultimately discharged on postoperative day 18 with a JP drain, which was removed at her 6-week follow-up once drain amylase normalized.

Given the high-risk pathologic features (pN2 disease, SMAD4 loss, extensive lymph node involvement), she was initiated on adjuvant gemcitabine plus capecitabine (GemCap) per the ESPAC-4 protocol at approximately 8 weeks post-surgery. Her starting CA 19-9 was 22 U/mL. She received 3 cycles of GemCap with dose reductions: capecitabine reduced by 25% after cycle 2 for Grade 2 hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia with tenderness limiting daily activities), and gemcitabine reduced on day 8 of cycles 2 and 3 for Grade 2 cytopenias (ANC nadir 0.9 x10^9/L, platelet nadir 88 x10^9/L). Nutritional status has been a persistent concern; albumin was 3.1 g/dL at her cycle 3 assessment.

The midpoint restaging CT (obtained after cycle 3 of GemCap) showed no locoregional recurrence but identified two subcentimeter hypodensities in the right hepatic lobe (segments VI and VII, 6 mm and 5 mm) not present on prior scans, with a CA 19-9 that had risen slightly to 41 U/mL. MRI demonstrated a 9 mm segment VI lesion and a 7 mm segment VII lesion with rim arterial enhancement, washout on portal venous phase, and hypointensity on hepatobiliary phase -- imaging features highly suspicious for metastatic disease in this clinical context. CA 19-9 at the time of MRI had risen further to 58 U/mL. She then underwent CT-guided core needle biopsy of the segment VI hepatic lesion. Pathology confirmed metastatic adenocarcinoma morphologically and immunohistochemically consistent with a pancreatic primary (CK7+, CK20-, CDX2-, CA19-9+, MUC1+, SMAD4/DPC4 negative, consistent with her index tumor).

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Diagnosis:
Ms. Choi is a 59-year-old female with Stage IV (M1, hepatic) pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, confirmed by core needle biopsy of a hepatic lesion identified during adjuvant chemotherapy. Her disease has now progressed beyond the surgical resection to involve the liver, and the current hepatic metastases were identified while actively receiving adjuvant GemCap -- indicating that her tumor has progressed through gemcitabine and capecitabine. In the context of her prior neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX exposure (8 cycles), she has now received two meaningful chemotherapy regimens.

The molecular profile of this tumor offers essentially no currently approved targeted therapeutic options: KRAS p.G12V (KRAS inhibitors currently approved in this space target G12C, not G12V), MSS/pMMR (checkpoint inhibitors such as pembrolizumab are not indicated), TMB-low (4 mut/Mb), no BRCA1/2 pathogenic variants, no NTRK fusions, no HER2 amplification, no BRAF mutation. SMAD4 loss is a marker of aggressive biology and has been associated with a propensity for metastatic dissemination. This tumor is biologically high-risk.

The multidisciplinary tumor board reviewed her case this week. Key points of discussion:

1. Prior therapy summary: (1) mFOLFIRINOX x 8 cycles (neoadjuvant) -- good initial response, then chemoradiation; (2) Whipple R0; (3) adjuvant GemCap x 3 cycles -- disease progressed on therapy.

2. Treatment options at this juncture were reviewed in detail with the patient and her family. Available second-line regimens for gemcitabine-refractory PDAC include:
   a. Nanoliposomal irinotecan (nal-IRI, Onivyde) plus leucovorin/5-fluorouracil (5-FU/LV) -- supported by the NAPOLI-1 trial data demonstrating improvement in overall survival in gemcitabine-refractory PDAC. This is a reasonable option given she is not irinotecan-naive (received irinotecan as part of prior FOLFIRINOX), though the nanoliposomal formulation has a distinct pharmacokinetic profile.
   b. FOLFOX (oxaliplatin, leucovorin, 5-FU) -- supported by retrospective data and the CONKO-003 trial in the second-line setting. However, given her prior oxaliplatin exposure across 8 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX and current residual Grade 1-2 bilateral peripheral neuropathy, re-challenging with oxaliplatin carries significant risk of worsening neurotoxicity and was discussed with caution. Cumulative neurotoxicity remains a meaningful concern.
   c. Clinical trial -- strongly considered; our research team will evaluate current enrolling trials. Given the molecular profile (KRAS G12V, no actionable alterations by standard criteria), enrollment in any available second-line PDAC trial including KRAS-targeting or stromal-remodeling strategies should be pursued in parallel.
   d. Best supportive care/palliative care focus -- discussed openly with the patient. At this time, she expresses a clear desire to pursue active treatment.

3. After discussion of the above options, weighing the patient's desire for active therapy, her performance status (ECOG 1, currently able to perform all self-care), relatively intact organ function (mildly elevated transaminases and alkaline phosphatase from hepatic metastases, but normal bilirubin and adequate renal function), the decision was made to proceed with nanoliposomal irinotecan (Onivyde) plus leucovorin/5-FU/LV (NAPOLI-1 regimen) as second-line therapy.

4. CRITICAL NOTE regarding UGT1A1 genotype: UGT1A1 *28/*28 status was identified prospectively. Per institutional protocol and published guidance for nal-IRI, the starting dose will be reduced from the standard 70 mg/m2 to 50 mg/m2 to mitigate the substantially elevated risk of Grade 3-4 neutropenia and diarrhea in homozygous *28 patients. 5-FU dosing will be per standard NAPOLI-1 protocol (2400 mg/m2 CIV over 46 hours), with leucovorin 400 mg/m2. We will monitor closely for toxicity and perform dose modifications as needed.

5. Palliative care: A formal palliative care consultation has been placed today. The patient and her family were supported by our oncology social worker during today's visit. The goals-of-care conversation was initiated in this visit -- Ms. Choi understands that this disease has now become metastatic, that cure is no longer possible, and that the focus of treatment is to extend life and maintain quality of life for as long as possible while managing symptoms. She expressed acceptance of this framing, though she and her family are understandably distressed. She does not currently have a completed POLST form; this will be addressed at a future visit or through the palliative care team.

6. Nutritional support: Continued PERT (Creon 72,000 lipase units with meals) for exocrine insufficiency. Referral re-activated to oncology dietitian. Albumin is trending down to 2.9 g/dL, which is concerning. Oral nutritional supplementation (Ensure Plus or Boost) will be strongly encouraged; if weight continues to decline, reassessment for enteral support will be considered. Cachexia in the setting of progressive PDAC is a significant driver of quality-of-life compromise.

7. Diabetes management: HbA1c 8.9% on basal-bolus insulin. This is suboptimally controlled and the hyperglycemia will worsen with any further dexamethasone use as antiemetic. Endocrinology/diabetology will be re-engaged for insulin regimen optimization. Metformin was discontinued at the time of Whipple procedure given post-pancreatectomy status.

8. Peripheral neuropathy: Currently Grade 1-2 at baseline. Given that we are transitioning away from oxaliplatin, this will hopefully remain stable. Gabapentin may be considered for symptom management if neuropathy worsens.
